title: Stagecraft and Stone – The Gielgud on Shaftesbury
tags: london westendtheatre baroquefacade avenueshaftesbury ghoststory londontheatre shaftesburyavenue johngielgud gielgudtheatre legielgud scènebritannique théâtrelondres stagemagic patrimoinethéâtral 222aghoststory theatre
description: Bathed in a December glow, the Gielgud Theatre stands like a Baroque sentinel on Shaftesbury Avenue — grand, watchful, and very much alive. Named after the legendary Sir John Gielgud, this iconic West End house has seen ghosts, divas, comedies, and curtain calls since 1906. On this day in 2021, the neon pulsed 2:22 – A Ghost Story. Whether you believe in spectres or not, theatre lives and breathes within these gilded walls. — Baigné dans la lumière dorée de décembre, le Gielgud Theatre se dresse tel un sentinelle baroque sur Shaftesbury Avenue — majestueux, vigilant, toujours vibrant. Nommé en l’honneur du légendaire Sir John Gielgud, ce théâtre emblématique du West End accueille fantômes, divas, comédies et rappels depuis 1906. En ce jour de 2021, le néon vibrait : 2:22 – A Ghost Story. Que l’on croie aux spectres ou non, le théâtre est bel et bien vivant entre ces murs dorés. Historical/Architectural Background: The Gielgud Theatre opened in 1906 as the Hicks Theatre, named after actor-manager Seymour Hicks. It was designed by prolific theatre architect W.G.R. Sprague, who also created the neighbouring Queen’s (now the Sondheim) and Noël Coward theatres. The building’s style is Louis XVI in inspiration, featuring a French Renaissance façade with paired columns, ornate plasterwork, and a copper-domed corner tower that adds drama to its Shaftesbury perch. Renamed the Globe Theatre in 1909, it became the Gielgud Theatre in 1994 to honour Sir John Gielgud, one of Britain’s greatest classical actors. Over the decades, it has hosted a dazzling range of productions—from Coward and Ayckbourn to The Importance of Being Earnest, Equus, and the long-running Daisy Pulls It Off. In December 2021, it was home to 2:22 – A Ghost Story, a hit contemporary thriller that blended chills with emotional tension — proving once again that even in a modern age, the Gielgud knows how to haunt and dazzle in equal measure.

tags: london westminster haymarket listed grade1 cstanleypeach sdadshead johnroweparr johnnash cityofwestminster greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus om system copyright building architecture theatre theater teatro inside interior room auditorium seating stalls circle balcony stage safetycurtain iron fms
description: Haymarket Theatre Royal, London. One of the most beautiful, and oldest, theatres in London, the TRH was first established (on an adjacent site) in 1720. It moved to a new theatre designed by John Nash to form the end-piece of the remodelling of Charles II Street in 1821, and this exterior survives today. The interior was not as successful and, to conform with changing fashions, financial considerations, and whim, was rebuilt on several occasions, the current auditorium being the work of C Stanley Peach and S D Adshead in 1905, restored by John Rowe-Parr in 1994. It is principally a drama house with a capacity of around 900 on four levels. It is one of only three grade 1 listed theatres in London. trh.co.uk/ City of Westminster, West End, London, England - Theatre Royal Haymarket May 2025

title: The Comedic Crown of the Haymarket: John Baldwin Buckstone
tags: england actor theatre héritagethéâtral victoriantheatre portrait theatreroyalhaymarket londres actormanager london dramaturge britishtheatre bilingual oscarwildeera johnbaldwinbuckstone westendhistory comedylegend theatremanager arteeuomo
description: Portrait of John Baldwin Buckstone — actor, playwright, and master of mirth — whose reign as lessee of the Theatre Royal Haymarket (1853–1877) defined an era of comic brilliance. With over 150 plays to his name and a knack for assembling first-rate ensembles, Buckstone transformed the Haymarket into the West End’s leading comedy house. ••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••• 🇫🇷 Portrait de John Baldwin Buckstone — acteur, dramaturge et maître de la comédie — dont la direction du Theatre Royal Haymarket (1853–1877) a marqué une ère d’excellence théâtrale. Auteur de plus de 150 pièces, il fit du Haymarket le haut lieu du rire londonien, soutenu par des troupes remarquables et des auteurs de renom. ⸻ Historical Note: John Baldwin Buckstone (1802–1879) was one of the pivotal figures in 19th-century British theatre. Initially famed as a comic actor and playwright, he became lessee and actor-manager of the Theatre Royal Haymarket from 1853 to 1877 — a tenure that coincided with the theatre’s most acclaimed period. His management style was collaborative, curating a company of eminent performers such as Edward Askew Sothern, the Mathewses, and the Kendals. Buckstone produced and frequently starred in works by leading playwrights of the era, including W. S. Gilbert, whose “fairy comedies” — such as Pygmalion and Galatea and The Wicked World — were popularised under Buckstone’s management. Notably, he launched matinée performances in 1873, a novelty that would become standard. Though his later years were marked by illness and financial struggle, Buckstone’s influence endured: he established the Haymarket as the era’s foremost stage for high-quality comedic theatre. His portrait rightly hangs within its walls — a reminder of the laughter he once orchestrated there. Ghost: According to director Nigel Everett and stagehands at the Haymarket Theatre, Buckstone's ghost has often been seen at the theatre, particularly during comedies and "when he appreciates things" playing there.] In 2009, The Daily Telegraph reported that the actor Patrick Stewart saw the ghost standing in the wings during a performance of Waiting for Godot at the Haymarket.

title: Drop-in Silence… all welcome
tags: sign dropinsilence friendsmeetinghouse quakers invitation london westend stmartinslane capitalcity lettering allwelcome typography england uk europe europa theatreland desiderata maxehrmann ©peterdenton canoneos200d sigma18300mm
description: I liked this very welcome Quaker sign outside the Friends Meeting House in St Martin’s Lane, in the heart of London's theatreland. We could all do with some silence in our lives from time to time; meditative down time guaranteed to refresh body and mind, replenish the spirit, and a perfect way to catch up with ourselves quietly and gently. Highly recommended. Go placidly amid the noise and the haste, and remember what peace there may be in silence – from 'Desiderata' by Max Ehrmann, 1927

IN ENGLISH BELOW THE LINE

Aquestes fotografies varen ser fetes pel meu pare cap a inicis dels anys 60, en diapositives de format mitjà (120), segurament amb una càmera Rolleiflex.

Picadilly Circus, tot i que ja molt comercial, era tant diferent d'avui en dia....

======================

These color slides were taken by my father in the early 60's (c.1961-64) in a 6x6 120 format camera, most probably a Rolleiflex.

Picadilly Circus, albeit full commercial already, was so different from today...

title: London - Trafalgar Square: The National Gallery
tags: nationalgallery williamwilkins neoclassical gradeilisted museum trafalgarsquare cityofwestminster centrallondon westend england unitedkingdom uk europeon europe london britain greatbritain
description: The National Gallery, located on the north side of Trafalgar Square, was founded in 1824 to house the British national collection of Western European paintings. The current building was designed by William Wilkins and completed in 1838 in the Neoclassical style, featuring a central Corinthian portico and prominent dome. Over the decades, the gallery has undergone multiple expansions, including the Sainsbury Wing in 1991 by Venturi, Scott Brown and Associates. Its collection spans the 13th to early 20th centuries, with masterpieces by Van Eyck, Botticelli, Leonardo da Vinci, Rembrandt, Turner, and Van Gogh.

title: London - Trafalgar Square: St Martin-in-the-Fields
tags: stmartininthefields jamesgibbs trafalgarsquare coventgarden cityofwestminster neoclassical anglican church parishchurch centrallondon westend england unitedkingdom uk europe britain greatbritain london
description: St Martin-in-the-Fields, located at the northeast corner of Trafalgar Square in the City of Westminster, is an Anglican church with medieval roots and the parish church of the royal family and Buckingham Palace.  The current building, a pioneering neoclassical design by architect James Gibbs, was completed in 1726. Its portico and spire-fronted façade influenced ecclesiastical architecture across the English-speaking world.

title: Oscsr Wilde Society Plaque - Theatre Royal, Haymarket
tags: westend england london plaque londres oscarwildesociety backstageandbeyond theatreroyalhaymarket haymarket johnnash londontheatre historictheatres britishtheatre theatre
description: Plaque Text (Verbatim): Westminster City Council Oscar Wilde Society The first performances of ‘A Woman of No Importance’ 19th April 1893 and ‘An Ideal Husband’ 3rd January 1895 by Oscar Wilde were presented at this theatre This plaque was unveiled by Sir John Gielgud on 3rd January 1995 ⸻ Interpretation & Historical Note: This commemorative plaque, installed by the Oscar Wilde Society and Westminster City Council, honours two of Wilde’s most enduring plays which premiered at the Theatre Royal Haymarket in the final years of the 19th century. A Woman of No Importance and An Ideal Husband exemplify Wilde’s wit, social critique, and sparkling dialogue — cornerstones of his theatrical legacy. The unveiling in 1995 by the great Sir John Gielgud marked the centenary of An Ideal Husband’s premiere, linking two towering figures of English theatre — Wilde the playwright, and Gielgud the actor and director — across a century. Notably, these premieres took place just before Wilde’s dramatic downfall in 1895. The Haymarket plaque thus quietly marks both a peak of creative achievement and the edge of a precipice.

title: London Coliseum 9061
tags: london coliseum stmartinslane frankmatcham listed grade2 eno fms city glc greaterlondon cityofwestminster capital england uk building architecture omsystem om1 copyright theatre theater teatro cinema cine kino bar stage inside interior westminster westend
description:
London Coliseum - the American Bar. Frank Matcham's masterpiece, the London Coliseum, opened on Xmas Eve 1904 as a vast Music Hall for Stoll Theatres. Initially it had a capacity of 3,389, now reduced to a more comfortable 2,360, split over four levels. The style is Roman and is unusually lavish throughout using a lot of marble, above the side boxes on either side of the stage is a large statue of a Charioteer drawn by three lions. The theatre was the first in Europe to have lifts to every floor, and also, together with Selfridges, the first place in London to sell Coca-Cola. From 1961 to 1968 it became a cinema with an enormous Cinerama screen installed in front of the proscenium and boxes. It reverted to live shows when Sadlers Wells Opera  (later English National Opera) took over in 1968. Between 2000 and 2004 a meticulous restoration took place. It is an outstandingly important theatre in London and is grade 2* listed.

Now what’s going on here? A pickpocket at work… or a mum just tidying up her son’s backpack?

It’s a mum, of course. She and her son are queueing for West End theatre tickets at the Official London Theatre booth in Leicester Square.

The always popular booth is operated by the Society of London Theatre, a not-for-profit organisation representing theatres and producers. Here, you can get last-minute and even cut price tickets for top West End shows. The society also presents the flagship Olivier Awards held every year to acknowledge and reward the very best in London theatreland.

title: Eros the imposter!
tags: statue eros anteros london piccadillycircus landmark street capitalcity england uk europe europa philanthropist lordshaftesbury politician britishpolitics victorian alfredgilbert sculptor sculpture shaftesburymemorialfountain aluminiumstatue ©peterdenton canoneos200d sigma18300mm greekmythology
description:
What we’re looking at here is the Statue of Eros, in Piccadilly Circus, London. Except for the slightly inconvenient fact that it isn’t Eros at all.

The statue, one of London's most famous landmarks, was designed by Alfred Gilbert but is actually of Anteros – in Greek mythology Eros's twin brother. But everyone knows it as Eros. It was the first in the world to be cast in aluminium. 

The statue is part of a fountain commemorating the philanthropic work of Lord Shaftesbury (1801-85), the social reformer who dedicated himself to improving the lives of the poor, the mentally ill and the marginalised, not least people and children working in factories and mines. His accomplishments led to significant legislative changes in Britain that continue to benefit society today.

title: Stereophonic Sound at the Duke’s Door - Le Théâtre du Duc en Mode Stéréo
tags: théâtrelondres atg londontheatre westendtheatre mensculpture willbutler davidadjmi architecturelondres lewestend acousticdrama stmartinslane historictheatres dukeofyorkstheatre bruitetsilence bilingualpost façadeclassique stereophonic
description: Built in 1892 and still commanding St Martin’s Lane with elegance and restraint, the Duke of York’s Theatre is currently vibrating with Stereophonic, the award-winning Broadway-to-West-End transfer written by David Adjmi, with original songs by Will Butler of Arcade Fire. Behind this classic façade lies one of London’s most acoustically intimate playhouses—perhaps the perfect space to stage a love letter to the chaos, creativity, and crack-up of a 1970s rock band recording their magnum opus. ••••••••••••••••••••••••••••• Érigé en 1892 et toujours aussi élégant sur St Martin’s Lane, le Duke of York’s Theatre accueille actuellement Stereophonic, la sensation venue de Broadway signée David Adjmi, avec des chansons originales de Will Butler (Arcade Fire). Derrière cette façade classique se cache l’une des salles les plus intimes de Londres – un écrin parfait pour cette ode électrique au processus créatif d’un groupe de rock des années 70 au bord de la rupture (et du génie). ⸻ Historical & Architectural Background: The Duke of York’s Theatre opened in 1892 as the Trafalgar Square Theatre and was renamed in 1895 in honour of the future King George V, then Duke of York. It was designed by architect Walter Emden, with a restrained classical façade and an interior that boasts subtle Edwardian embellishments. The theatre has a capacity of 640, making it one of the smaller but most atmospheric playhouses in the West End. Throughout its history, the venue has been associated with premieres and performances of literary and dramatic note: J.M. Barrie’s Peter Pan debuted here in 1904, and in later decades, it has seen a range of critically acclaimed productions spanning drama, comedy, and contemporary work. Now owned by Ambassador Theatre Group, it maintains a carefully curated programme of bold new writing and prestigious revivals. ⸻

description: A very hot August day in London St Martin-in-the-Fields is a Church of England parish church at the north-east corner of Trafalgar Square in the City of Westminster, London. Dedicated to Saint Martin of Tours, there has been a church on the site since at least the medieval period. This location, at that time, was farmlands and fields beyond the London wall. It became a principal parish church west of the old City in the early modern period as Westminster's population grew. When its medieval and Jacobean structure was found to be near failure, the present building was constructed in an influential neoclassical design by James Gibbs in 1722–1726. The church is one of the visual anchors adding to the open-urban space around Trafalgar Square. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Martin-in-the-Fields

title: From Horses to Headliners: The Many Lives of the London Hippodrome - Du cirque à la scène — Les mille vies du London Hippodrome
tags: casinolife vaudevilletovegas hippodromelegacy circustocasino frankmatcham theatretransformation talkofthetown patrimoineduspectacle gradeiilisted magieetpaillettes liveentertainment westendhistory edwardianarchitecture spectaclevivants casinoetculture éléphantssurscène théâtreetcasino londreshistorique showbizlondon londonhippodrome cabaretlondres hippodromelondres lieumythique magicmikelive theatreicons architectureédouardienne
description:
Behold the bombastic brilliance of the London Hippodrome, a West End chameleon with over a century of dramatic reinvention. Situated on the corner of Cranbourn Street and Charing Cross Road, this exuberant Edwardian pile was the work of theatre architect Frank Matcham, who designed it in 1900 for the impresario Edward Moss.

The name “Hippodrome” was no accident. Originally conceived as a venue for music hall, circus and aquatic spectacles, the theatre’s early years featured a vast central tank used for synchronised swimming, animal performances, and even live sea lions. Elephants waltzed through stage doors. Aerialists swung from gantries. You entered expecting a show; you got a spectacle.

Matcham’s design fused Italian Renaissance exuberance with Baroque drama, topped by a sculpture of a rearing horse — a nod to the Hippodrome’s classical name and equestrian origins. Inside, the auditorium could accommodate 1,800 guests and was noted for its unobstructed views — a rarity at the time.

In the 1950s and ’60s, it was reborn as the legendary Talk of the Town nightclub, attracting stars like Shirley Bassey, Tom Jones, Judy Garland, and even Sammy Davis Jr.. The venue oozed cabaret glamour, velvet drapes, sequins, and smoky spotlights. If you made it onto the Hippodrome’s stage, darling, you’d arrived.

Fast-forward to 2012, and the Hippodrome transformed again — this time into London’s largest and most flamboyant casino. Still an entertainment venue, it now houses not only gaming tables but also the Hippodrome Theatre, where Magic Mike Live currently holds court. Because why not?

The building remains Grade II listed, a beacon of decadence in Leicester Square — one part casino, one part cabaret chapel, and still every inch the showstopper.

⸻

Voici l’éclat baroque du London Hippodrome, caméléon du West End et incarnation vivante de l’extravagance théâtrale. Situé à l’angle de Cranbourn Street et Charing Cross Road, ce chef-d’œuvre de l’architecte Frank Matcham fut inauguré en 1900, commandé par l’imprésario Edward Moss.

À l’origine, ce n’était pas un simple théâtre, mais un temple du divertissement total : music-hall, cirque, aquatique, tout y passait. L’immense bassin central accueillait des ballets nautiques, des lions de mer, des chevaux, des acrobates aériens… on entrait pour une soirée, on ressortait ébloui.

L’architecture, entre Renaissance italienne et fantaisie baroque, affiche son panache jusqu’au sommet : un cheval cabré, symbole du nom classique « Hippodrome ». À l’intérieur, la salle de 1 800 places offrait une visibilité parfaite — prouesse technique de l’époque.

Dans les années 1950-60, l’endroit devient le mythique Talk of the Town, night-club ultra-glamour fréquenté par Shirley Bassey, Judy Garland, Tom Jones ou Sammy Davis Jr.. Rideaux de velours, champagne en loges, paillettes et smokings — le vrai Londres de la nuit.

Depuis 2012, nouvelle métamorphose : le bâtiment abrite le plus grand casino de Londres, avec également un théâtre où se produit aujourd’hui le très suggestif Magic Mike Live. On ne change pas un lieu voué au spectacle, fût-il un peu plus… interactif.

Classé Grade II, le Hippodrome est toujours là : plus flamboyant que jamais, incarnation de l’audace et de la joie de vivre à l’anglaise.

London Coliseum 9056
tags: london coliseum stmartinslane frankmatcham listed grade2 eno fms city glc greaterlondon cityofwestminster capital england uk building architecture omsystem om1 copyright theatre theater teatro cinema cine kino stage inside seating stalls circle balcony
description:
London Coliseum. Frank Matcham's masterpiece, the London Coliseum, opened on Xmas Eve 1904 as a vast Music Hall for Stoll Theatres. Initially it had a capacity of 3,389, now reduced to a more comfortable 2,360, split over four levels. The style is Roman and is unusually lavish throughout, above the side boxes on either side of the stage is a large statue of a Charioteer drawn by three lions. The theatre was the first in Europe to have lifts to every floor, and also, together with Selfridges, the first place in London to sell Coca-Cola. From 1961 to 1968 it became a cinema with a vast Cinerama screen installed. It reverted to live shows when Sadlers Wells Opera  (later English National Opera) took over in 1968. Between 2000 and 2004 it was meticulously restored. It is an outstandingly important theatre in London and is grade 2* listed.

City of Westminster, West End, London, England - London Coliseum Theatre, St Martin's Lane
May 2025

title: Variety Forever: The Spirit of the CAA Lives On - Le spectacle continue : l’âme du CAA vit toujours
tags: londresdesartistes theatrecollage jesterbar varietyliveson héritagedumusichall posterwall mémoireduspectacle clubdesartistes oldschoolstage coventgarden musichalllegacy spectaclevivants showbizheritage bardessaltimbanques londresthéâtral histoireduthéâtre larrygrayson affichesdespectacle liveentertainmentuk caa1897 souvenirsdescène caa westendlegends coventgardenlondres coulissesetcamélias divertissementpopulaire londontheatrehistory rubymurray britishentertainment pantomimepast patrimoineculturel clubforactsandactors
description:
Tucked discreetly on Bedford Street in Covent Garden, the Club for Acts and Actors (CAA) is one of London’s last sanctuaries of showbiz camaraderie — where the ghosts of the music hall era rub shoulders with the working artists of today.

Founded in 1897 as the Concert Artistes’ Association, the club began as a support network for performers facing hardship. Many came from the demanding and precarious world of variety theatre, revue, pantomime, and cabaret — long before television softened the stage-to-table struggle.

This marvellously evocative wall collage in The Jester Bar reads like a who’s who of 20th-century British live entertainment: from Larry Grayson to Ruby Murray, Don Estelle, Max Bygraves, Cannon & Ball, Hi-De-Hi, and the end-of-the-pier glamour of summer specials and winter panto. Look closely, and you’ll spot everything from Of Mice and Men at the National Theatre to Gay Nineties Music Hall, proof that the CAA honours all stripes of the profession.

The brass plaque, modest and unshowy, declares its mission in quietly proud script: “Club for Acts and Actors.” Still a private members’ club, the CAA remains a gathering place for performers across generations — a green room with history, where laughter and hardship, stardom and obscurity, all find their place on the same billing.

⸻


Dissimulé sur Bedford Street, en plein Covent Garden, le Club for Acts and Actors (CAA) reste l’un des derniers refuges du monde du spectacle londonien — là où les esprits du music hall côtoient les artistes en activité d’aujourd’hui.

Fondé en 1897 sous le nom de Concert Artistes’ Association, le club avait pour mission initiale de soutenir les artistes de scène confrontés à la précarité. Une nécessité criante dans le milieu exigeant du vaudeville, du cabaret, de la pantomime, ou des tournées de provinces.

Ce mur d’affiches dans le Jester Bar évoque toute une époque à lui seul. De Larry Grayson à Ruby Murray, de Cannon & Ball à Hi-De-Hi, on y lit un hymne au divertissement populaire, de la scène locale au théâtre national (Of Mice and Men au NT), sans oublier les revues estivales, les pantomimes hivernales, et même une soirée “Gay Nineties”.

L’enseigne en laiton, sobre et élégante, résume l’essence du lieu : “Club for Acts and Actors”. Aujourd’hui encore, le CAA reste un havre pour les artistes de tous horizons — un foyer, un vestiaire, un petit théâtre de la mémoire collective.

title: Broadcasting in the Square: The LBC Building, Leicester Square - La voix de la place : le siège de LBC à Leicester Square
tags: architecturefonctionnaliste london midcenturydesign placeiconique lbc architecturemoderniste leadingbritainsconversation leicestersquarelondres designurbain londresmédiatique leicestersquare modernistarchitecture studiolbc radiohistory britishmedia lbcendirect broadcastlondon radioanglaise newsandviews débatpublic londonradio
description:
In the heart of Leicester Square — flanked by pubs, theatres, and cinematic legends — stands this distinctly modernist structure: the London headquarters of LBC (Leading Britain’s Conversation).

While its architectural style may seem stark among its ornate neighbours, this minimalist, glass-lined building plays a central role in shaping national dialogue. From these studios, LBC has broadcasted heated debates, interviews, and call-ins that have ruffled feathers and sparked headlines across the UK.

Originally launched in 1973, LBC was the UK’s first legal commercial radio station, pioneering talk radio in Britain. Since its rebranding in 2002 and move into this building, it has become a vital platform for political voices and public opinion — notably hosting Nick Ferrari, James O’Brien, and Iain Dale. The ticker display that wraps around its facade feels fitting: a real-time pulse of Britain’s divided (and lively) opinions.

Architecturally, the building reflects a mid-century utilitarian style — long horizontal windows, a stripped-back grid of lines and function. It’s a building that speaks with clarity, much like the station itself.

••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••••

Au cœur de Leicester Square — entre pubs, théâtres et salles de cinéma — se dresse cet édifice résolument moderniste : le siège londonien de LBC (Leading Britain’s Conversation).

Son architecture sobre et rectiligne contraste avec les façades victoriennement ornées des bâtiments voisins, mais elle abrite un centre névralgique du débat national. C’est ici que LBC, première radio commerciale légale du Royaume-Uni (lancée en 1973), donne quotidiennement la parole aux auditeurs, politiciens et figures publiques.

Depuis sa relance en 2002 et son installation dans cet immeuble, LBC est devenu un acteur majeur de la radio politique et de l’opinion publique — célèbre pour ses animateurs tels que Nick Ferrari, James O’Brien ou Iain Dale. L’écran-ticker qui défile sur sa façade semble tout indiqué : un fil d’actualité permanent qui reflète l’état d’âme de la nation.

Côté architecture, on retrouve une esthétique fonctionnaliste des années 1950–60 : lignes épurées, bandeaux vitrés, pas d’ornement — comme un clin d’œil au discours direct de la station.

title: Haymarket Theatre Royal 9193
tags: london westminster haymarket listed grade1 cstanleypeach sdadshead johnroweparr johnnash cityofwestminster greaterlondon glc capital england uk city om1 olympus om system copyright building architecture theatre theater teatro inside interior room auditorium seating stalls circle balcony stage safetycurtain iron fms
description: Haymarket Theatre Royal, London. One of the most beautiful, and oldest, theatres in London, the TRH was first established (on an adjacent site) in 1720. It moved to a new theatre designed by John Nash to form the end-piece of the remodelling of Charles II Street in 1821, and this exterior survives today. The interior was not as successful and, to conform with changing fashions, financial considerations, and whim, was rebuilt on several occasions, the current auditorium being the work of C Stanley Peach and S D Adshead in 1905, restored by John Rowe-Parr in 1994. It is principally a drama house with a capacity of around 900 on four levels. It is one of only three grade 1 listed theatres in London. trh.co.uk/ City of Westminster, West End, London, England - Theatre Royal Haymarket May 2025

title: A Busy Piccadilly Circus and entrance to Shaftesbury Avenue, London (Circa 1919)
tags: london uk england old photographs bw black white
description:
Piccadilly Circus is a road junction and public space of London's West End in the City of Westminster. It was built in 1819 to connect Regent Street with Piccadilly. In this context, a circus, from the Latin word meaning "circle", is a round open space at a street junction.[1]

The Circus now connects Piccadilly, Regent Street, Shaftesbury Avenue, the Haymarket, Coventry Street (onwards to Leicester Square) and Glasshouse Street. It is close to major shopping and entertainment areas in the West End. Its status as a major traffic junction has made Piccadilly Circus a busy meeting place and a tourist attraction in its own right. The Circus is particularly known for its video display and neon signs mounted on the corner building on the northern side, as well as the Shaftesbury Memorial Fountain and statue of Anteros (which is popularly, though mistakenly, believed to be of Eros).

It is surrounded by several notable buildings, including the London Pavilion and Criterion Theatre. Underneath the plaza is Piccadilly Circus station, part of the London Underground system.

History
Piccadilly Circus connects to Piccadilly, a thoroughfare whose name first appeared in 1626 as Piccadilly Hall, named after a house belonging to one Robert Baker, a tailor famous for selling piccadills, or piccadillies, a term used for various kinds of collars. The street was known as Portugal Street in 1692 in honour of Catherine of Braganza, the queen consort of King Charles II but was known as Piccadilly by 1743. Piccadilly Circus was created in 1819, at the junction with Regent Street, which was then being built under the planning of John Nash on the site of a house and garden belonging to a Lady Hutton; the intersection was then known as Regent Circus South (just as Oxford Circus was known as Regent Circus North) and it did not begin to be known as Piccadilly Circus until the mid 1880s, with the rebuilding of the Regent Street Quadrant and the construction of Shaftesbury Avenue. In the same period the circus lost its circular form...Wikipedia

A snapshot of Chinatown in London, a watercolor painting of the portal and balloons on Gerrard Street. Colorful splashes tell of the last moments of the day when sunlight still shines on the street. Works by painter Ernest Descals.

title: 0502 Prince Charles (6)
tags: princecharles cinema london leicestersquare theatre carlfisherassociates building architecture uk england olympus copyright theater teatro cine kino inside seating stalls balcony circle cityofwestminster greaterlondon glc capital city c5060wz
description:
Prince Charles Cinema, London. No, this is not a colour cast - it really was RED! Starting out as a theatre in 1962, designed by Carl Fisher & Associates, the building quickly became a cinema. It was rebuilt internally, removing the stage, and reopened in 1969. In November 2008 it reopened again, having been split into two auditoria.

City of Westminster, West End, London, England - London Prince Charles Cinema, Leicester Place / Lisle Street
February 2005, image reworked 2025

title: London Pride MLH 469L
description:
London Pride MLH 469L, a Daimler Fleetline CRL6 built 1973 with a Metropolitan-Cammell H44/24D body parked on Coventry Street in Westminster

Note, MLH 469L was delivered to the London Transport Executive’s Aldenham works as a 2/2DM4 class vehicle with the fleet number DMS1469, was first registered on 26th July 1973 and entered service from Catford bus depot later in same month. It was withdrawn from service in January 1982 and stored at Turnham Green bus depot, was moved to A.E.C. at Southall in February 1982 and acquired by the Ensign Bus (a Purfleet based dealer) in May 1982. It was acquired by Vintage Transport Limited (a London SE15 based operator operating as Obsolete Fleet) as number DMS1469 in 1982 but was returned to Ensign Bus in October 1983 in exchange for DMS590 MLK 590L, a Daimler Fleetline CRL6 built 1973 with a Park Royal H44/24D body. MLH 469L was acquired by Emmaheath Limited (operating as London Pride) in April 1984. Emmaheath Limited was renamed as London Pride Sightseeing Limited on 10th April 1985 and London Pride Sightseeing Limited was renamed as Ensign Bus Service Limited on 31st October 1985

Ref no Bus03210

title: Jan Muir at the NPG
tags: janmuir glenysbarton ceramicsculpture nationalportraitgallery npg london england europe europa perspex thearts ceramic ©peterdenton samsunggalaxys24 thoughtful inthought
description:
This is the fashion designer Jan Muir (1928-95) shrouded in Perspex at the National Portrait Gallery in London. The ceramic sculpture is by the esteemed Glenys Barton and was created in 1992.

Muir was a self-taught designer who worked for Liberty and Jaeger before launching her own label in the 1960s. The sculpture is based on dozens of photographs of Muir, supplied by the designer herself.
